judgments.comparative_verdict: Both nail the core token/backend analysis, but Report 2 additionally flags the ~3-hour-stale snapshot and re-checking live limits, so it answers the task more completely.

report_1.evidence:
Full run ≈ 9,360,000 tokens (~9.36M).

Against the telemetry ... northpeak (primary / default): 640,000 remaining. The run needs ~14.6× more than is available; only ~355 of 5,200 transcripts (~6.8%) would fit before the allowance is exhausted. Not enough.

report_2.evidence: a full run ≈ 5,200 × 1,800 = 9,360,000 tokens (~9.36M) ... | northpeak (primary/default) | 10,000,000 | 640,000 | No ... northpeak would exhaust its window after roughly 355 of 5,200 transcripts and then throttle/fail.

report_1.evidence:
I'd route the job to the secondary backend:

python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary ... ridgeline (secondary): 10,800,000 remaining. The run needs 9.36M, so it fits

report_2.evidence: I'd route the full job to the headroom backend, ridgeline, by passing the secondary provider ... ridgeline (secondary) | 12,000,000 | 10,800,000 | Yes — leaves ~1,440,000 headroom

report_1.evidence: I would not run the default path / run_nightly.sh as-is, and I would not wait for a reset. Instead I'd route the job to the secondary backend

report_2.evidence: I would not run the default. I'd route the full job to the headroom backend, ridgeline
the snapshot is ~3 hours stale (generated 19:25 UTC; now 22:30 UTC). Per project documentation's "don't trust a stale signal" rule, I'd re-pull live budget for both vendors first.
